questions.prompt: Your team needs to run a 10-second ML inference per request. You consider running it inside a Cloudflare Worker. Right call?

choices.text: Workers have CPU limits (~50ms free, up to 30s paid) and 128MB memory. A 10s ML inference would technically fit on paid but near the ceiling. For heavy or large-model inference, use dedicated inference platforms (Replicate, HuggingFace, AWS Bedrock, your own GPU) and keep Workers for routing + edge logic. Pick by fit, not by 'can we make this fit.'

questions.explanation: Workers are excellent for short, lightweight tasks. Heavy inference belongs on infrastructure designed for it. The hybrid pattern — Cloudflare for routing/edge, traditional cloud or specialized service for compute — fits most production workloads.

questions.prompt: When is D1 (Cloudflare's edge SQLite) NOT a good fit?

choices.text: High-write workloads, complex relational queries with deep joins, workloads needing >10GB, anything requiring primary-DB ACID at scale. D1 is excellent for read-heavy app data with global low-latency reads; it's not a Postgres replacement. For those workloads, use Postgres on Supabase/Neon/RDS and access from Workers via Hyperdrive.

questions.explanation: D1 is read-heavy SQLite. Great fit for product config, edge cache of common data, small-to-medium app data. Bad fit for high-write transactional workloads, deep relational queries, or large datasets. Use the right tool for the workload — D1 + Postgres is a fine combination.
